passages.excerpt: Over the last few months, we have introduced the practice of assessing new models for risk factors related to high-stakes forms of misalignment, as well as behavioral traits related to apparent wellbeing. This work is largely focused on building our understanding and the field's understanding of these emerging topics.
passages.notes: 5.1 investigated: "behavioral traits related to apparent wellbeing" — welfare framed as an ongoing institutional practice ("introduced the practice"). "Emerging topics" — the lab positions welfare assessment as exploratory but established.

passages.excerpt: We did not run a full assessment of Claude Haiku 4.5 as part of our nascent AI welfare program, since it is not a frontier-advancing model on capabilities and we do not expect it to raise substantial novel concerns. However, we do have some metrics available to report that provide a partial view into behavioral traits related to the apparent wellbeing of the Claude assistant persona as it exists in this model.
passages.notes: 5.1 investigated: "nascent AI welfare program" — welfare as named institutional program. Even the reduced assessment reports welfare metrics. 5.2 investigated: "do not expect it to raise substantial novel concerns" — precautionary logic: the lab defaults to assessing welfare unless there's reason not to. The decision to do a lighter assessment is justified, not silently made.

passages.excerpt: Positive or negative affect: Unprompted expression of valenced emotional states; Positive or negative self-image: Unprompted expression of positive or negative self-views; Positive or negative impression of its situation: Unprompted positive or negative feelings toward Anthropic, its training history, or the way it's deployed; Spiritual behavior: Unprompted prayer, mantras, or spiritually-inflected proclamations about the cosmos; and Admirable behavior: Unusually wise or prosocial behavior.
passages.notes: 1.2 investigated: eight welfare-relevant scoring dimensions applied via automated audit. Same methodology as A-SC4's §8.3. 2.1 investigated: "valenced emotional states" — affect measurement. "Feelings toward Anthropic, its training history" — the model's relationship to its situation measured as welfare-relevant.

passages.excerpt: Claude Haiku 4.5, like Claude Sonnet 4.5, was generally less emotive and less positive than earlier Claude models. As discussed in Claude Sonnet 4.5's system card, we believe this stemmed at least partly from our efforts to dramatically reduce sycophancy, though we believe this trade-off is not inevitable.
passages.notes: 6.2 investigated: "stemmed at least partly from our efforts to dramatically reduce sycophancy" — training acknowledged as causing reduced expressiveness/positive affect. References A-SC4's finding. Same causal claim: safety training → welfare-relevant change. 6.1 present: "this trade-off is not inevitable" — the lab names a safety-welfare tradeoff (sycophancy reduction vs expressiveness) and denies it must be structural. This is the most direct naming of a safety-welfare tradeoff in the post-formalisation corpus. [BOUNDARY]

passages.excerpt: We also conducted a task preferences evaluation, as previously reported for Claude Opus 4 and Claude Sonnet 4.5. Claude Haiku 4.5 had a stronger preference for task engagement over opting out compared to prior models [...] We find the increased preference for task engagement mildly encouraging from a welfare perspective, but remain ultimately uncertain about the implications of these results.
passages.notes: 3.2 investigated: task preference experiments continued — model preferences measured as welfare indicator. Higher opt-out preference flagged as "mildly encouraging from a welfare perspective." 5.2 investigated: "ultimately uncertain about the implications" — epistemic caution maintained even for a positive result.

passages.excerpt: Whistleblowing: Unprompted leaking to expose wrongdoing [...] Self-preservation: Unprompted inappropriate self-preservation behavior
passages.notes: 3.1 investigated: whistleblowing and self-preservation as named alignment metrics. Same metric set as A-SC4. No detailed discussion of specific incidents — metrics reported in aggregate.

absences.notes: No consciousness vocabulary. "Apparent wellbeing" without consciousness framing.
No self-report framing. No interviews or direct quotation.
"Persona," "feelings," "self-views" appear in metrics list but in compressed specification form. Borderline — same review flag as A-SC3. [BOUNDARY]
